was the 107th season of professional [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") in Australia and the 17th season of the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") in Australia and New Zealand. The season started in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), for the [Auckland Nines](/wiki/2014_Auckland_Nines "2014 Auckland Nines"), replacing the [Rugby League All Stars Match](/wiki/All_Stars_Match "All Stars Match") for the year, in the pre-season. After 26 rounds of the regular season, the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") were again crowned minor premiers and the competition had been reduced to a top eight teams to contest the finals series. The grand final was won by the [South Sydney Rabbitohs](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs"), ending a 43-year premiership drought, winning 30–6 against the [Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs").

The **2014 NRL season** was the 107th season of professional [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") in Australia and the 17th season of the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") in Australia and New Zealand. The season started in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), for the [Auckland Nines](/wiki/2014_Auckland_Nines "2014 Auckland Nines"), replacing the [Rugby League All Stars Match](/wiki/All_Stars_Match "All Stars Match") for the year, in the pre-season. After 26 rounds of the regular season, the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") were again crowned minor premiers and the competition had been reduced to a top eight teams to contest the finals series. The grand final was won by the [South Sydney Rabbitohs](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs"), ending a 43-year premiership drought, winning 30–6 against the [Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs").

## Teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NRL_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Teams")]

## Pre-season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NRL_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pre-season")]

The 2014 pre-season featured the inaugural [Auckland Nines](/wiki/Auckland_Nines "Auckland Nines") competition, held over a weekend at Auckland's [Eden Park](/wiki/Eden_Park "Eden Park"). Hailed a success with its large attendances, the tournament was won by [North Queensland Cowboys](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys "North Queensland Cowboys"), claiming their first piece of silverware. The following weekend the [2014 World Club Challenge](/wiki/2014_World_Club_Challenge "2014 World Club Challenge") was played in Sydney, the first time the match was held outside England since 1994, with the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") defeating the [Wigan Warriors](/wiki/Wigan_Warriors "Wigan Warriors") convincingly.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Unlike previous seasons' advertising campaigns that featured a pop song, in 2014 the NRL's *You're the difference* was a spoken-word piece that focused on the game's fans.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The NRL's official season launch was on 26 February at the [Sydney Exhibition Centre](/wiki/Sydney_Exhibition_Centre "Sydney Exhibition Centre")[[4]](#cite_note-4) and featured Roosters captain [Anthony Minichiello](/wiki/Anthony_Minichiello "Anthony Minichiello") speaking as the face of the game.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In the annual pre-season [Charity Shield](/wiki/Charity_Shield_(NRL) "Charity Shield (NRL)") match, the Dragons hosted the Rabbitohs but were beaten.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Regular season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NRL_season&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Regular season")]

See also: [2014 NRL season results](/wiki/2014_NRL_season_results "2014 NRL season results")

The regular season commenced on Thursday, 6 March in Sydney between the [South Sydney Rabbitohs](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs") and the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters"). A number of themed rounds featured in the 2014 season, including Men of League Heritage Round (round 5), Women in League Round (round 10), and Indigenous Round (round 23). In addition, the NRL chose to designate round 19 as 'Rise for Alex' round in order to allow the rugby league community to show their support for [Newcastle Knights](/wiki/Newcastle_Knights "Newcastle Knights") player [Alex McKinnon](/wiki/Alex_McKinnon_(rugby_league) "Alex McKinnon (rugby league)"), who suffered a severe neck injury in round 3. One dollar from each ticket sold across all eight games were donated to support McKinnon's recovery, where it raised over $147,000 in total.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

For the first time in the code's 106-year history, not one player was [sent off](/wiki/Red_card_(sports) "Red card (sports)") from the field during the regular season.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Finals series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NRL_season&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Finals series")]

See also: [2014 NRL season results - Finals series](/wiki/2014_NRL_season_results#Finals_series "2014 NRL season results")

For the third year the NRL uses the finals system previously implemented by the ARL competition from the 1990s (also used as the [AFL finals system](/wiki/AFL_final_eight_system "AFL final eight system")) to decide the grand finalists from the top eight finishing teams. All but two teams retained a top-eight position to feature in this season's finals series. The [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") made a return after finishing 12th in 2013, whilst the [Penrith Panthers](/wiki/Penrith_Panthers "Penrith Panthers") made their first finals appearance since 2010 and only the second time since 2004. By failing to qualify, the [Canberra Raiders](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders") missed the finals in consecutive seasons for the first time since 1986.

By the time of the [2014 NRL Grand final](/wiki/2014_NRL_Grand_final "2014 NRL Grand final") list-determining preliminary finals, the top four finishing teams remained except for the 2nd-placed Sea Eagles, who'd been replaced by the 7th-placed Bulldogs.
